pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that all meaningful upside in life is located on the other side of uncertainty and delay, and these are the only two prices the market systematically refuses to pay. Because both uncertainty (not knowing the outcome) and delay (not knowing how long one must persist) feel unbearable to most people, they represent the primary source of arbitrage for anyone willing to bear them.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi asserts that once an entrepreneur fully understands all the hidden costs and failure modes of their current opportunity — having lived through the valley of despair — they can begin solving those problems one by one and the difficulty begins to decrease, making persistence past that point disproportionately valuable.

pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi states that all a business is, reduced to its most basic unit, is an economic arbitrage between what it costs to acquire more revenue and the difficulty of continuing to do that — meaning the only meaningful optimization target is return on capital (financial and human) per unit of effort.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi claims that the hardest part of any pursuit is not the beginning (getting started) but continuing to do unglamorous repetitive work once the initial excitement fades and rewards are not guaranteed. pairs.b_text: According to Alex Hormozi, consistency is the rarest of human traits because it is structurally invisible: you cannot observe another person's consistency unless you are already consistent yourself, since you must be present every day in the same environment to witness their daily presence.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that serially jumping from opportunity to opportunity at the first sign of difficulty is the primary reason entrepreneurs fail to achieve large outcomes: each jump restarts the cycle at uninformed optimism, meaning the entrepreneur never passes through informed pessimism and the valley of despair to reach the upside on any single path.

Low fortitude produces a large, steep drop in behavior quality (e.g., quitting a job, major life disruptions); high fortitude produces only a small, imperceptible shift before the person regains normal behavior.

pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that serially jumping from opportunity to opportunity at the first sign of difficulty is the primary reason entrepreneurs fail to achieve large outcomes: each jump restarts the cycle at uninformed optimism, meaning the entrepreneur never passes through informed pessimism and the valley of despair to reach the upside on any single path.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a primary ad platform (Google Ads) goes down unexpectedly during a high-stakes campaign, the correct immediate response is to re-route the entire budget to the next platform (Meta), then sequentially to the next (TikTok, then LinkedIn) until spend is restored — rather than waiting for the failed platform to recover.
